topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the silent, brutal finality of a closing coffin lid. *Event Cancelled.* My thumb hovered over the 'reply' button, a tremor running through my hand that I couldn't seem to quell. A party? My surprise party? The carefully constructed anticipation I’d been nurturing for weeks evaporated, leaving behind a vast, echoing emptiness in my chest. I reread the subject line three times, the words blurring into meaningless static. I shot up from the sofa, pacing the length of the living room like a caged animal. My breath hitched in ragged gasps, and I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to physically stop the flood of disbelief. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and brittle in the quiet apartment. "No, this isn't happening."

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the pixelated text mocking me with its indifference. The silence in the room was deafening, broken only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs. My hands began to shake so violently that I had to grip the edge of the desk to keep them from flying open. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I let out a sharp, involuntary sound—a choked, high-pitched noise that felt entirely foreign. I dropped the phone onto the wooden floor with a clatter, the sound seeming impossibly loud. Every nerve ending in my body was screaming; my stomach twisted into a tight, painful knot, and I started breathing in shallow, rapid bursts, unable to draw a full, steady breath. I began to run in tight circles, my vision tunneling, convinced that the world was tilting on its axis.

[story 3]

When the confirmation arrived, it felt less like news and more like a physical blow. I stood frozen by the window, watching the streetlights flicker on, the mundane activity below seeming utterly detached from the seismic shift happening inside me. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles protesting beneath my skin. I started pacing, a tight, jerky motion that spoke volumes about the internal chaos raging within. I kept touching my face, my fingers tracing the line of my jaw, searching for some tangible proof that this was a dream, a cruel trick of the light. A sudden, sharp intake of air caught in my throat, followed by a desperate need to move, to scream, but no sound emerged. My legs felt weak, unsteady, and I stumbled back against the wall, my entire body vibrating with a frantic, uncontrolled energy.
